data.title: Mueller Water Products: Secular Growth Tailwinds, Margin Expansion Potential And Attractive Valuation Support A Buy
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I last covered Mueller Water Products (MWA) in December last year with a buy rating, and the stock is up a nice ~15% since then, outperforming the S&P 500’s (SPY) ~10% gains. Moving forward, the company looks well positioned for revenue growth, driven by steady demand in municipal repair and replacement of aging water systems, including lead service line replacement projects tied to federal funding. On top of that, a recovery in the residential market as interest rate cycles turn/reverses, and the carryover impact of recent pricing actions into FY26 should give sales another boost.

Margins also have room to move higher. The closure of the legacy brass foundry is already driving cost savings and efficiency gains, with more to come as production fully transitions and we see a full year of benefit in the numbers next year. Add in operating leverage from higher volumes, ongoing productivity improvements, and carryover pricing, margin trajectory looks constructive.

Despite these drivers, the stock trades at a discount to both its own history and peers in the water infrastructure space. With earnings growth ahead and a clear case for multiple re-rating, the setup looks attractive. I continue to rate MWA Stock a Buy.

Revenue Analysis and Outlook

Mueller reported a strong top-line performance in the last quarter, with revenue up 6.6% year over year to $380.3 million. This was driven by higher volumes and pricing across most product lines. The growth was broad based, with contributions coming from both Water Flow Solutions (WFS) and Water Management Solutions (WMS).

In WFS, sales went up 4.1% to $216.6 million, supported by stronger volumes in iron gate and specialty valves along with gains in the pricing across the majority of product lines. This was partially offset by lower brass shipments as backlogs normalized and channel inventories came down. WMS performed even better, posting sales up 10.2% to $163.7 million, helped by robust demand for repair products and hydrants combined with higher pricing. Natural gas distribution saw lower volumes, which generated some pressure, which is also tied to backlog normalization, but the strength in repair more than offset the pressure.

Overall, the company reported healthy momentum, with both segments contributing to growth and price realization holding firm against the backdrop of backlog normalization in certain product categories.

Looking forward, revenue outlook for Mueller looks solid, which is supported by long-term demand trends as well as some near term catalysts. Mueller’s biggest end market - municipal repair and replacement, which accounts for 60-65% of sales—is holding up well. Even with the interest rates high, cities are funding critical water distribution projects, indicating that the need to replace aging water infrastructure just isn’t optional.

The IIJA-related water infrastructure funding has been sluggish early this year due to compliance issues under the Build America, Buy America provisions. Nonetheless, it represents a multi-year opportunity. The political support is present, and the increased focus on water quality keeps funding on the agenda. In the near term, with regulatory clarity emerging, I expect the projects to see a pickup. Management noted that many communities are now moving ahead with lead service line replacement programs, which was a key focus of the IIJA-related infrastructure funding. Management also flagged that states such as California, Texas, Colorado, and Minnesota have put water-related funding measures on their ballots, which could bring more regional demand sooner.

On the residential side, which makes up around 25-30% of sales, things are going differently. High mortgage rates and builder caution are keeping the pressure on single-family starts. But the Fed is likely to start cutting the rates, which could ease up financing, and housing activity should start to recover. Muller’s products are used in new community development, hence any pickup in single family housing should flow directly into their order book.

Another factor that might support the revenue going into FY26 is carryover pricing. The company rolled out a broad increase in February 2025 and later added targeted hikes on specialty and repair products to offset tariffs. Given that these were implemented midway through the fiscal year, FY25 seizes only a part of the benefit. A full carryover impact will be seen in FY26, giving the company a progressive rise in reported sales even without any new pricing actions.

Overall, the combination of secular drivers, which were aging water systems, state-level funding, and a pickup in federal infrastructure disbursements, should set a strong base for growth. Add a possibility in housing recovery as rates come down, and it feels Mueller is in a good position to deliver a revenue momentum build as we move into FY26.

Margin Analysis and Outlook

Mueller’s gross margin expanded 150 bps year-over-year to 38.3%, helped by manufacturing efficiencies, higher volumes, and pricing actions, which more than offset tariff headwinds. However, adjusted EBITDA margins didn’t do as well and fell 120 bps to 22.7%, largely due to a $7.7 million FX drag from the Israeli shekel.

Segment-wise, Water Flow Solutions’ adjusted EBITDA margin decreased 110 bps to 31.0%, but this was primarily due to tough comparison from the prior year. The margins improved sequentially in this segment, making me optimistic about margin increases in coming quarters as comps ease. Water Management Solutions posted a 21.6% adjusted EBITDA margin, down from 22.9% a year ago, with FX again the key headwind. Stripping out the currency impact, the segment actually showed underlying improvement, indicating that operational execution remains solid even against a messy external backdrop.

Moving forward, I am optimistic about Mueller’s margin outlook. The closure of the legacy brass foundry is the biggest near-term driver. That step has removed the duplicate costs of running two facilities simultaneously, which involved labor, overhead, and operational inefficiencies, while shifting more production to the new silicon-based, lead-free foundry. The new plant has more automation and runs with better throughput; hence, it carries a built-in cost advantage. The company already started to see its benefit in Q3, and management has talked about an 80-100 basis point margin benefit in the back half of FY25, with complete year impact rolling through in FY26.

In addition, higher volumes should also benefit margins. With demand expected to remain healthy, better absorption of fixed costs should create good operating leverage. The company also continues to invest in its iron foundries and modernization projects, so the productivity gains should add some incremental support as well.

Another factor is pricing. The broad price increases from February 2025 and targeted hikes on specialty and repair products are still flowing through, and management said that the benefit from carryover pricing will extend into FY26. So, that should help.

Putting it all together, the setup for FY26 margins looks favorable. Foundry shift, carryover pricing, and operating leverage should all support expansion. Tariffs remain unpredictable, but if we look at its last quarter gross margin, the company has already shown it can mitigate the impact through pricing and supply chain moves, which leaves them in a good position to deliver margin gains over the next year.

Valuation and Rating

Mueller is trading at 20.9x FY25 consensus EPS of $1.26 and 18.9x FY26 consensus EPS of $1.40. On FY26 numbers, which are more relevant this late in the fiscal year, the stock trades at a discount to its five-year average forward P/E of 21.7x. The discount looks notable given the setup with both near-term catalysts and long-term demand drivers lining up well.

Against water infrastructure peers, the gap is even clearer. Tetra Tech (TTEK) is trading at a P/E of 23.78x FY26 EPS, while Xylem (XYL) is at 26.06x FY26 EPS. By comparison, Mueller sits at a meaningful discount, despite having visibility into steady municipal demand, a likely recovery in residential construction as rates fall, and secular growth from aging water infrastructure replacement.

Given this good growth visibility, the current valuation discount doesn’t feel justified, and I see a good chance of the stock reverting to its historical average forward P/E. The company is set up for earnings growth, driven by carryover pricing, foundry cost savings, and operating leverage from higher volumes. Municipal spending provides a defensive base, while residential offers cyclical upside as financing costs ease. Federal funding, should also accelerate as regulatory clarity improves. Put together, it’s not hard to see a path for both EPS growth and some multiple re-rating, which in combination points to solid upside from current levels.

Risks

Mueller’s growth is closely dependent on government spending on municipal water infrastructure. Although the need for funding is strong, and aging systems along with high costs of water loss make investment hard to ignore, any delays in disbursing funds by the government could weigh on near-term growth trajectory.

I am expecting the interest rate cycle reversal to catalyze further growth as project financing costs in municipal and mortgage payments in residential should reduce, helping demand. However, if the pace of rate cuts disappoints, the growth acceleration I am expecting may not materialize.

data.title: QDTE: The Fund's Performance Shows An Investment Built For Adverse Conditions
data.description:
A good investment has to be able to withstand difficult conditions. While many companies and funds will obviously do well in a more favorable market environment, tough times usually show more about a financial choice, and show which options are built to last.

Covered call funds have evolved significantly over the last decade, and some of the newer options-based investments have utilized new and more effective strategies that offer investors both stable income and substantive total returns as well.

One increasingly preferred covered-call fund that has performed well over the last year is the Roundhill Innovation-100 0DTE Covered Call Strategy ETF (QDTE).

QDTE has offered investors total returns of 26.44 percent since the fund's inception in March of 2024, while the Nasdaq-100 index that this fund tracks has offered investors total returns of 29.07 percent during the same time period.

I last wrote about the Roundhill Innovation-100 0DTE Covered Call Strategy ETF in March of 2025, and I rated the fund a strong buy. I wanted to upgrade my coverage of this fund since there is limited data on this investment, and the market has been more volatile over the last six months. Today, I am reiterating my strong buy rating. QDTE has performed very well over the last 6 months, even in more adverse market conditions. The ETF's recent performance shows this fund's strategy can offer investors a good balance of income and total return even in a more challenging economic environment.

The Roundhill Innovation-100 0DTE Covered Call Strategy Fund has an expense ratio of .97 percent, $886.59 million in assets under management, and a trailing yield of 39.14 percent. The fund tracks the Nasdaq-100 index with synthetic call spreads, and also buys government treasuries with income received from selling covered calls. This ETF makes weekly payouts, and the payouts generally have tax advantages since these distributions are usually taxed as a return of capital.

The options strategy that QDTE uses is that the ETF uses call spreads where the fund buys a deep-in-the-money call and then during the day it sells 0-days-to-expiration index call options with a delta of around 15-25 that are close to at-the-money during the day, which implies a nearly 75 percent chance of these daily contracts expiring worthless each day.

Almost all of the gains in the S&P 500 (SPY) have occurred during overnight and pre-market hours in the morning since 1993, so QDTE has applied this data to the fund's strategy in taking a long position in the Nasdaq-100 index. The fund's strategy of not holding covered calls overnight has helped this investment offer investors solid total returns and preserve the net asset value of the ETF as well.

QDTE has also been able to pay out consistent income with the fund's strategy. This ETF has paid out $13.52 of income per share in the last year and $20.85 per share of income since the investment's inception in March of 2024.

QDTE has been consistently paid out between $.14 and $.24 a share of weekly income, with distributions usually in a tighter range between $.20 a share and $.23 a share. The ETF's strategy has worked even during the more volatile months of March and April of 2025.

QDTE has also performed well overall, both during the more challenging market conditions earlier in the year and compared to other peers.

QDTE has outperformed the JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPQ), and the Global X NASDAQ 100 Covered Call ETF (QYLD), over the last year, and the fund also did not sell off as hard as those other investments during March and April of 2025. This ETF has also captured most of the upside in the underlying Nasdaq-100 index the fund tracks, even with the harsh sell-off in early 2025.

All investment theses have risks, and QDTE will likely underperform the Nasdaq-100 index this fund tracks over the long term since the ETF's covered call strategy is fairly conservative during the day. There is also limited data for this fund since QDTE's inception date was in March of 2024. This ETF has still not been tested during an extended and more severe sell-off, although the empirical data suggests the strategy the investment uses should limit downside risks in that scenario, as was seen during the more volatile months of March and April.

There are a number of different covered-call funds that are becoming increasingly appealing that utilize a variety of more sophisticated strategies than the original, more basic ones used by these types of funds a decade ago. QDTE's approach of leaving the positions long positions uncovered during the day has worked well over the last year, even in more adverse conditions, and the ETF's recent results should give income investors looking for a good balance between income and total returns more confidence.

data.title: Bath & Body Works: Structural Weakness And Visible Margin Pressure (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Summary

I upgraded to a hold rating for Bath & Body Works Inc. (BBWI) in June, as I wanted to see if the new CEO would be able to catalyze a rejuvenation in growth. The 2Q25 results, however, confirm my skepticism. While sales and EPS landed at the high end of guidance, underlying demand weakness, inventory swell, and margin pressures suggest a business that is still struggling. I downgrade to sell.

Earnings results update

On the surface, BBWI's 2Q25 results looked fine, but when you look deeper, it reveals a business that is losing momentum. As a recap, net sales came in at $1.55 billion, up 1.5% y/y, and EPS of $0.37 landed at the high end of guidance. But that’s where the positives stop. Growth actually slowed sharply from 2.9% y/y in 1Q25, confirming my concern that last quarter’s strength was inflated by the one-off Disney collaboration. With that tailwind gone, the underlying softness of the business is showing again. Profitability was equally underwhelming. Gross margin did expand by 30 bps y/y, but this came from leverage on occupancy costs after closing a fulfillment center, not from healthier product sales or pricing power. That small gain was completely offset by the SG&A increase, which grew 110 bps y/y due to higher healthcare costs and stepped-up investments. As a result, operating income actually fell 6% y/y. The bottom line is that BBWI’s results highlight a business relying on cost adjustments to meet near-term targets, while its core demand and category strength continue to weaken.

Core Business Challenges

Interestingly, the most telling evidence that BBWI is facing deeper problems came directly from new CEO Daniel Heaf. He admitted that BBWI has failed to acquire enough new customers and is not connecting with the younger consumers. As much as I appreciate this honesty here, the fact of the matter is that it validates that the BBWI brand is losing relevance. As the current core customer base ages, the lack of younger shoppers is a structural weakness. Without the ability to organically acquire new customer acquisition consistently, BBWI is basically left relying on large-scale, one-off events to drive growth, a strategy that is neither sustainable nor reliable over the long term.

To make things worse, the new CEO highlighted another significant issue—new consumers often find the in-store assortment “overwhelming,” which discourages precisely the younger shoppers BBWI needs most. This problem is even more apparent in digital, which led to the 10.1% y/y decline in 2Q25. Management attributed some of the weakness to the shift toward Buy Online, Pickup In Store (BOPIS), but even after adjusting for that, direct sales were still down 3% y/y. While BBWI has plans for a new app and refreshed website scheduled for later this year, I am not so hopeful they will deliver tangible results based on the execution so far. In my view, the problem is not just a web and a new app; it’s the lack of a strategy to serve as the primary bridge to younger demographics. I would imagine BBWI will need to drastically go through a rebrand or spend significant resources on marketing and product refresh to make this work. For now, it just doesn’t seem like anything will happen to close this gap.

Inventory Situation Goes from Bad to Worse

The inventory situation at BBWI is now a major red flag. In 2Q25 the inventory buildup has accelerated sharply: inventory ended the quarter up 13% y/y, nearly nine times faster than the 1.5% y/y sales growth. This is a steep deterioration from the ~7% y/y increase I flagged in 1Q25 and highlights that the imbalance is worsening, not improving. In absolute terms, inventory has swelled to $977 million. Management continues to mention that the buildup was due to tariff-related pull-forwards and insists inventory remains healthy. But when guidance for 3Q25 calls for just 1%–3% y/y sales growth, the numbers tell a different story. The widening gap between inventory growth and expected sales makes it increasingly likely that BBWI will be forced into heavier promotional activity to clear shelves. This is especially concerning when we know that BBWI likely needs to go through a drastic change to attract younger demographics (would this include a major product assortment swap? This would mean BBWI needs to clear inventory. As I warned previously, this is the exact scenario that drags down the bull case. Deeper discounting would directly pressure gross margins and set up a poor second-half earnings performance.

Intensifying Margin Pressure and a Cautious Consumer

Looking ahead, the path to stabilizing operating margins also looks increasingly difficult. On the external side, tariffs represent the most immediate challenge. Management guided to an FY25 net negative impact of ~$85 million, with the bulk of the pain concentrated in 3Q25. That quarter alone is expected to absorb a $40 million hit, which equates to ~240 bps of pressure on the gross margin. The drag continues into the holiday season as well, with another ~100 bps impact expected in 4Q25. At the same time, internally, overhead costs are trending higher. SG&A continues to increase due to strategic investments under the new CEO. On this end, management has raised its FY25 SG&A guidance, which points to a weaker operating margin outlook than what was guided just a quarter ago.

The timing of both of these couldn’t be worse. To put everything together, BBWI is facing structural weakness in its core Body Care category, which makes it hard to either pass along higher costs or ease off promotional activity without risking further sales decline. This combination of rising costs and a demand weakness puts BBWI in an extremely tight spot and, in my view, casts serious doubt on whether margins can even improve in the near term.

Valuation

BBWI trades at 8.3x NTM PE today, ~3x lower than the past 5-year average of 11x. I believe this discount is justified given the visible weakness in both the near and long terms. I believe as BBWI continues to show weaknesses in demand, lack of strategy to reach out to younger demographics, and margin gets pressured from a weak inventory position, the valuation multiple will continue to fade lower, potentially back to ~6.8x—lows in June. That would represent ~20% downside.

Investment Risk

The key upside risk is that the new CEO’s initiatives deliver faster traction than I expected. The upcoming digital upgrades and expanded distribution through 600+ college bookstores could help the brand finally resonate with younger consumers. At the same time, the recently announced multi-year Disney partnership has the potential to be more than just a one-off boost, providing a more durable lift to sales.

Conclusion

In conclusion, my rating for BBWI is a downgrade back to sell. BBWI’s problem with its core customer base is a structural weakness that it has not been able to fix. That, along with inventory swelling far ahead of sales and with visible margin headwinds ahead, I think the stock will face a lot of pressure in the coming months. Until BBWI proves it can connect with younger consumers and stabilize fundamentals, I see downside risk.

data.title: The U.S. Must Rebuild Domestic Uranium Enrichment Capacity
data.description:
By Daniel Maguire, ACA

Introduction

As hyperscalers turn to nuclear power for clean, reliable energy to support growing demand for AI-related compute, President Trump has signed an Executive Order to quadruple US nuclear capacity by 2050.1 Accounting for up to ~50% of uranium fuel costs, enrichment has become a critical bottleneck.2 Once the sole provider of nuclear enrichment in the West,3 the US now has virtually no commercial enrichment capacity. Europe, Russia, and China now dominate, as shown below. According to our research, a looming demand-supply imbalance highlights the urgent need to invest in domestic enrichment capacity, not only to enable AI but also to bolster energy independence and meet 2050 goals.

What Is Uranium Enrichment?

Uranium enrichment is a critical step in the production of nuclear power. Natural uranium contains ~0.7% of the fissionable isotope U235,5 far less than that needed for reactor fuel. Enrichment increases the U235 concentration, with requirements varying by application, as shown below. Advanced reactors can require U235 levels roughly ~20x higher than that in natural uranium.

Enrichment is measured by Separative Work Units (SWU), the standard metric of capacity.6 Because of the minimal incremental effort required to move from advanced reactor-grade to weapons-grade uranium, as shown below, enrichment facilities are highly sensitive geopolitically and subject to strict oversight under international non-proliferation agreements. From a commercial standpoint, however, while enrichment above 5% requires only modest additional SWU, reaching 20% yields only ~26 kg of product from 1,000 kg of natural uranium feed—underscoring that SWU scales roughly linearly with desired output mass.

Historically, enrichment relied on energy-intensive gaseous diffusion. Today, gas centrifuges are much more efficient, and laser enrichment—still under development—promises significant improvements, though proliferation concerns could impede its path to commercialization, as shown below.

Uranium Enrichment Methods

Gas Diffusion Gas Centrifuge Laser Enrichment
Cost High Capital Cost ~1/10th The Capital Cost Of Diffusion Low Capital Cost
Energy Intensity kWh/SWU 2,500 50 ~<50
Separation Factor ~1.004 ~1.3 ≥50
Stages Required 500 50 1-2
Proliferation Risk Low Medium High
Activity Dormant Dominant Demonstration

Note: The separation factor measures how effectively a single enrichment stage increases U235 concentration. Higher-efficiency methods like centrifuge and laser require fewer stages than diffusion, enabling smaller, more concealable facilities that are harder to detect and could be used for covert weapons production. Source: ARK Investment Management LLC, 2025. This ARK analysis draws on a range of external data sources as of August 7, 2025, which may be provided upon request. For informational purposes only and should not be considered investment advice or a recommendation to buy, sell, or hold any particular security.

US Enrichment Capacity Has Declined Precipitously

With large-scale gaseous diffusion technology developed during the Manhattan Project, the US once led the world in uranium enrichment. As the Soviet Union and Europe developed more efficient centrifuge methods, the US launched the Gas Centrifuge Enrichment Plant (GCEP) in the 1970s to remain competitive. Unfortunately, the Three Mile Island crisis destroyed the appetite for nuclear power in the US, and then, as global markets moved into oversupply, the US cancelled GCEP in 1985 and began to rely on its aging diffusion plants,8 as shown below.

In the 1990s, the US privatized enrichment under the United States Enrichment Corporation (USEC), which relied on outdated diffusion and then pursued AVLIS10—a laser-based enrichment method that encountered debilitating technical hurdles.11 In 1993, a Megatons to Megawatts deal flooded the global market with cheap Russian enriched uranium, further discouraging US investment.12 In 2014, USEC declared bankruptcy, reemerging a few months later as Centrus Energy.13 Without industrial scale diffusion or commercial centrifuge projects, the US now is decades behind Russia, China, Germany, Netherlands, and the UK, as shown below.

Now Is The Time To Invest

ARK’s research suggests that, unlike forecasts in the 1980s, SWUs will be in short supply by 2030 thanks to rising demand from new nuclear buildouts, including Small Modular Reactors (SMRs), as shown below.

Geopolitical risks have catapulted the US into action. Executive Orders are channeling billions of dollars into rebuilding our nuclear fuel ecosystem,15 with companies like Centrus Energy, Global Laser Enrichment, General Matter, and LIS Technologies in line to benefit. That said, commercial-scale deployment will require investment, create significant execution risks, and require policy support to guarantee not only US energy independence but also AI dominance.

Conclusion

Rising energy demand and electrification are stimulating the resurgence of the nuclear industry in the US. Given the shortfall in global enrichment capacity likely by 2030, the US must rebuild domestic capacity to remain competitive. Not only will increased domestic capacity support hyperscalers seeking clean, reliable power and enable nuclear and SMR deployment, but it also will restore US leadership in the nuclear fuel cycle. Overcoming development and deployment challenges will require serious coordination between business and government, targeted policy support, and long-term investment. In our view, the US is gearing up for success.

Footnotes

  1. The White House, President Donald J. Trump. 2025. “ORDERING THE REFORM OF THE NUCLEAR REGULATORY COMMISSION.”

  2. World Nuclear Association. 2025. “Uranium Enrichment.”

  3. Pouris, A. 1986. “The future cost of uranium enrichment: Technology and economics.” Energy Policy.

  4. World Nuclear Association. 2025. “Uranium Enrichment.”

  5. An atom that can undergo nuclear fission, meaning its atoms can split when hit by a neutron releasing energy.

  6. SWU is a measure of the effort required to enrich uranium, defined by a complex equation that balances feed, product, and tails assays. Enrichers adjust the tails assay based on market conditions: when uranium is expensive and SWU is cheap, they underfeed by lowering the tails assay; when uranium is cheap and SWU is costly, they overfeed by raising it to reduce enrichment effort.

  7. World Nuclear Association. 2025. “Uranium Enrichment.”

  8. Waters, D.A. 2003. “The American Gas Centrifuge Past, Present, and Future.” Paper presented to the SPLG workshop.

  9. Pouris, A. 1986. “The future cost of uranium enrichment: Technology and economics.” Energy Policy.

  10. Atomic Vapor Laser Isotope Separation is a process that uses lasers to ionize specific isotopes selectively in a vaporized element for enrichment or separation.

  11. Waters, D.A. 2003. “The American Gas Centrifuge Past, Present, and Future.” Paper presented to the SPLG workshop.

  12. Megatons to Megawatts was a US-Russia program that ran from 1993 to 2013 and converted Russian weapons-grade uranium into fuel for US nuclear power plants.

  13. Centrus Energy Corp. 2025. “History.”

  14. Platov, M. 2025. “Uranium Enrichment.” WORLD NUCLEAR UNIVERSITY SUMMER INSTITUTE, Shanghai, China.

  15. U.S. Department of Energy. 2025. “HALEU Enrichment Services.” See also U.S. Department of Energy. 2024. “Biden-Harris Administration Announces Contracts to Buy U.S.-Sourced Low Enriched Uranium.”

Disclosures

ARK's statements are not an endorsement of any company or a recommendation to buy, sell or hold any security. For a list of all purchases and sales made by ARK for client accounts during the past year that could be considered by the SEC as recommendations, click here. It should not be assumed that recommendations made in the future will be profitable or will equal the performance of the securities in this list. For full disclosures, click here.

©2021-2026, ARK Investment Management LLC (“ARK” ® ”ARK Invest”). All content is original and has been researched and produced by ARK unless otherwise stated. No part of ARK’s original content may be reproduced in any form, or referred to in any other publication, without the express written permission of ARK. The content is for informational and educational purposes only and should not be construed as investment advice or an offer or solicitation in respect to any products or services for any persons who are prohibited from receiving such information under the laws applicable to their place of citizenship, domicile or residence.

Certain of the statements contained on this website may be statements of future expectations and other forward-looking statements that are based on ARK's current views and assumptions, and involve known and unknown risks and uncertainties that could cause actual results, performance or events to differ materially from those expressed or implied in such statements. All content is subject to change without notice. All statements made regarding companies or securities or other financial information on this site or any sites relating to ARK are strictly beliefs and points of view held by ARK or the third party making such statement and are not endorsements by ARK of any company or security or recommendations by ARK to buy, sell or hold any security. The content presented does not constitute investment advice, should not be used as the basis for any investment decision, and does not purport to provide any legal, tax or accounting advice. Please remember that there are inherent risks involved with investing in the markets, and your investments may be worth more or less than your initial investment upon redemption. There is no guarantee that ARK's objectives will be achieved. Further, there is no assurance that any strategies, methods, sectors, or any investment programs herein were or will prove to be profitable, or that any investment recommendations or decisions we make in the future will be profitable for any investor or client. Professional money management is not suitable for all investors. For full disclosures, please go to our Terms & Conditions page.

The Adviser did not pay a fee to be considered for or granted the awards. The Adviser did not pay any fee to the grantor of the awards for the right to promote the Adviser's receipt of the awards nor was the Adviser required to be a member of an organization to be eligible for the awards. data.title: Utz Brands: Steady Business In The Snacking Industry
data.description:
At one hundred and four years old, Utz Brands, Inc. (UTZ) has established itself as the third-largest company in the US for salty snacks. While the company is focused on a variety of snack categories, it has seen its salty snack category increase for the last eight consecutive quarters. Although the stock has seen better days, losing 27.07% of its value over the last five years, historically, the stock has rewarded investors with 39.36% in returns. In FY 2024, the company generated $1.4 billion in annual revenue and a net profit of $16 million, after two years of losses. One of the major changes was a large investment into a new distribution center. The management team is also focusing on marketing, both of which can lead to further sales increases, and they have been cutting out small-margin brands from their profile and closing ineffective operations.

While Utz may not be a standout business, I was attracted by the easy-to-understand consumer segment, a four-year steady dividend program, and the fact that the management team is taking an active approach to its brand portfolio and operations. Although it may not deliver high growth, the company holds on to a portfolio of strong brands, is showing upward financials, and its price-to-earnings ratio is lower than its five-year average, and it is trading cheaper than some of its larger peers. Therefore, investors may want to take a bullish stance on this stock.

Company overview and growth drivers

Utz Brands was founded in 1921 in Pennsylvania as a family business of making and selling chips to the local community. Four generations later, the family is still very much involved, even after going public via a SPAC in 2020. On the website, the company now has fifteen brands under its portfolio with a focus on selling salty snacks such as pretzels, potato chips, and popcorn. The company has four power brands, namely Utz, On the Border, Zapps, and Boulder Canyon. In 2020, the company acquired On the Border, a tortilla chip and salsa business, for $480 million. This increased Utz's position in the tortilla chip market and allowed the company to diversify into dips. There is a huge opportunity within the US salty snack industry, estimated at $30.3 billion in 2024, of which 95% of all adults take part in.

Although the company's top-line results have remained pretty flat for several years, there are a few growth drivers that I believe have the potential to increase the company's top-line results. The first one is that the company has a number of strong core brands, such as Boulder Canyon, Utz and Zapp's, all of which have claimed the number one spot in a few states across the USA. The company is using this brand strength to expand into new snack formats and follow customer preferences, such as the increase in healthy snacking trends. It is also expanding its reach into more states, such as Florida and western states.

The second growth driver that I want to mention is the distribution center that opened in 2024, which helps Utz Brands to better serve its customers. Additionally, the company has increased its marketing spend by 70% more than its original plan.

Lastly, the company is focusing on profitable brands and plants, and in this way, it has been divesting from low-margin brands and unproductive factories.

The company has shown clear signs of increasing its snack portfolio with popular brands, improving logistics, and focusing on high-margin products and operations, which are positive signs if you are considering investing in a company.

Financial overview

Utz Brands has experienced slow topline growth over the last three financial years, but it has improved its profitability in FY2024. The strongest sales performance is within its salty snack category. The company's gross profit has increased from its focus on higher-margin products and improvements within the company's operations.

In the table below, it is clear that profits grew sharply in FY2024 to $16 million after losses in 2023 and 2022. While improved margins helped profitability, investors should be aware that the company sold off businesses that gave them a one time benefit of $44 million, and there was a reduction in interest expenses.

In terms of cash flow, the company has delivered positive levered free cash flow over the last two financial years and has a positive free cash flow of $21 million on a twelve-month trending [TTM] basis. The company provides a dividend program with a yield of 1.82%, which is, however, lower than that of its peers in the industry. Furthermore, the cash flow allows the company to make some of the investments it has been making, such as the purchase of the new distribution center, without taking out more debt.

Unfortunately, and important to note, debt is one of the big red flags surrounding this business. Total debt stands at $1.05 billion. The company has a current ratio of 1.29, so it can cover its short-term liabilities, but total debt to equity is very high and should be a concern for potential investors at 76.51%. The company has high-interest expenses due to the debt, which cuts significantly into its profits.

Valuation

I have compared Utz to some of its peers in the snacking industry, namely J&J Snack Foods (JJSF), The Hershey Company (HSY) and Conagra Brands (CAG). If I look at the price-to-sales and price-to-earnings (P/E) ratios, Utz looks inexpensive versus brand-heavy peers like Hershey. The price-to-sales ratio of 0.79 indicates that investors are paying less than a dollar for every dollar earned. Its forward EV/EBITDA is higher than Conagra’s but lower than Hershey’s, indicating that there might be some growth potential. Analysts’ average price target is $17.10; at the current stock price, there is 27.71% upside potential, which is a positive sign for investors thinking about buying at this price.

Risks

There are several risks that investors should consider before they invest in Utz Brands. As discussed earlier, the company has a really high debt of $1.05 billion. We can see that larger peers in the industry also appear to have a high debt intake.

The interest expenses connected to debt have also taken a big slice out of the company's operating profit. If the company has a weaker year of performance, it may need to cut its dividend or reinvest to pay off the debt expenses. Another risk investors should consider is the relatively low profit margin on products. Again, if we compare it to peers in the industry, we can see that its margin is slightly above average. However, investors should remain cautious of the impact of potential cost increases in potatoes, labor, and fuel and the impact on the slim margins.

Final thoughts

Utz Brands is a steady, profitable, and cash-generating business in an easy-to-understand and high-demand snack industry. Its salty snack category is well-known and bought repeatedly. It is using the strength of its brands to expand into new product innovations and untapped geographic areas. This is not a stock for investors looking for a fast-growth stock. I think it could be interesting if you are looking for steady growth and a small dividend, especially if Utz keeps growing sales by a few percent per year and cutting costs as planned; its profit-per-share should rise. Although I am cautious of the importance of seeing debt reduced and margins ideally improving. The company is stable and showing active efforts to improve; therefore, I recommend a bullish stance on this stock.

data.title: SVOL: Downgrade To Hold On Strategy Shift
data.description:
Simplify Volatility Premium ETF (SVOL) is an income ETF that I covered last year in April and later in October. While I was very confident at the time, subsequent decisions by active management changed the risk profile, with negative results. I re-rate SVOL to Hold, sharing the lessons in my disappointment.

Summary Of Previous Theses

When I initially covered SVOL, their income strategy was the following:

  • Commit 20% to 30% of assets to shorting the Volatility Index (VIX)
  • Use long-dated VIX futures, buying back as they lose time value
  • Put most of the remaining assets in liquid Treasury positions
  • Commit a small portion to hedges to protect against VIX spikes

By shorting the VIX, SVOL necessarily correlates with the stock market.

With this strategy, SVOL was able to produce a steady 16% yield, and in the few years of its life, it had managed to beat the market. Nevertheless, it was more at risk from a sudden VIX spike causing its short position to rise in value than a stock portfolio would be. Learning from the 100% short VIX funds of the past (which got wiped out), their hedging would offer protection. In my first thesis, I ultimately concluded:

With a fixed-income portfolio of reliable credit, generally consistent trends of VIX futures pricing, and active management that can adjust at moments where the norm makes no sense, I have a hard time seeing where the yield trap is. Even disappointment would produce a high yield with this type of fund.

When I followed up in October, I remarked on a VIX spike that occurred later that summer, peaking at 40, but that hadn't managed to cripple their NAV.

Realizing this was an important stress that they had passed, I reaffirmed my buy rating.

Subsequent Events

SVOL ultimately did not deliver with the same durability that I thought it would after October. First, let's look at the capital losses.

While SVOL remains stable for the rest of 2024, the price began to decline in February and was down significantly in April.

True to its design as a VIX-sensitive fund, these bigger losses into 2025 resulted from VIX activity, which reached a greater peak than 2024's, breaching 50. This largely coincided with fears leading up to and the eventual implementation of President Trump's tariffs.

The income still being important, dividends declined from their normal $0.30 per month after August's VIX spike, reaching as low as $0.26 in December. They did not recover to $0.30 until May.

The primary contributor to the change appears to be the shift in their strategy out of Treasuries or Treasury-based funds.

If we look at the change in their holdings from March 2024 (left) to March 2025 (right), there was a shift out of their stable Treasury positions and into SPDR® S&P 500® ETF (SPY) and Simplify US Equity PLUS Upside Convexity ETF (SPUC), both of which are equity funds. Simplify Multi-Qis Alternative ETF (QIS) is a Simplify fund with no fixed strategy, regularly changing its positions.

I suspect this was largely a response to the rate cuts that occurred in late 2024 and guidance coming out of the year that more would occur. The equity positions would therefore benefit in addition to the positive correlation already posed by the short VIX strategy, as short-term Treasuries would not benefit from cuts.

At the same time, this was a much more volatile portfolio, and it increased the chances that NAV would not fare as well against a VIX spike, which is what happened.

Outlook and Valuation

The motives for such a shift are easy to understand. With it being an actively managed fund, it was also expected that they would make adjustments as they saw fit. Nevertheless, I believe one of the main selling points in my original coverage was their stable Treasury holdings and the benefit that would pose to NAV and an income investor's base of capital. It complemented the hedging portion of the strategy, while providing yield.

I believe we have to consider that management may position SVOL into more volatile assets again in the future and that this may make NAV difficult to maintain or recover when already beset by a VIX spike.

A sample of their recent holdings shows that positions in SPUC and QIS remain. Simplify Next Intangible Core Index ETF (NXTI) is another equity fund. SVOL is sticking to this strategy for now.

I personally believe that it is risky, and I am not sure if the $0.30 can be sustainable if NAV isn't as secure as it once was. A shrinking base of capital will eventually have to make a cut, and another VIX spike could lead to just that at this point.

While the return to $0.30 per month means the forward yield would be 21%, I have less confidence that they have the tools to protect their portfolio adequately from the VIX. This isn't the same as saying that they will go belly-up like the 100% short funds that preceded them, but it's enough to question the sustainability of the income.

Conclusion

My initial thesis was correct in the sense that investors would likely enjoy a strong yield on cost even with setbacks, which is what they got. I don't think it was correct enough. NAV is more than 20% lower, defeating any chance of reallocating favorably. It remains unclear how long SVOL can maintain that with the decreased share value, particularly if more turbulence occurs.

I think this is a learning lesson that is only fair to share with the readers. With that lesson, I think it only makes sense to downgrade SVOL to Hold.

data.title: Meta: A Big Overhang Could Dissipate In The Near Term
data.description:
I’m reiterating my Strong Buy rating on Meta Platforms (META) after a nice pullback at the end of August. I believe there is a non-zero chance a big overhang could dissipate in the near term; I'm talking about the FTC case.

The trial ran from April 14 to May 27, 2025, with no verdict yet, so uncertainty persists. I believe this uncertainty is an overhang on the stock, considering that one of the remedies could include the divestiture of Instagram and WhatsApp. The September 2 Google decision rejected the divestiture of Chrome and curbed exclusive default deals while allowing non-exclusive payments (not binding here, but directionally important for Big Tech stocks in the US).

Instagram is Meta's revenue engine. Analysts see this year’s US ad revenue at $32 billion, up 24.4% over 2024, with US ARPU around $250 versus Facebook at $207, TikTok at $132, and YouTube at $40. Instagram could have about 2 billion monthly users and 500 million daily users, so a forced sale would be a real topline hit.

WhatsApp, while not as important on a revenue contribution basis, is still a key part of Meta's ecosystem, especially in click-to-message ads run on Facebook and Instagram.

Overall, I see the P/E multiple as attractive, considering Meta's growth in the Family of Apps segment. Furthermore, resolving the FTC case could remove a big overhang on the stock, justifying a multiple expansion.

The FTC Overhang - The Good, The Bad, And The Ugly

One of the overhangs on Meta's stock is the FTC’s 2020 monopolization case over Instagram/WhatsApp. The case went on trial this year, between April 14 and May 27. So far, the court hasn't issued a verdict yet, which adds a big layer of uncertainty to investors, considering that one of the remedies could include the divestiture of the Apps.

I believe the chance of such an event is small (but not zero). Here is why.

On September 2, the judge overseeing the U.S. search monopoly case against Google rejected the DOJ’s request to force Google to divest Chrome. Google will be banned from exclusive default search deals (like the $20 billion deal with Apple in 2022 to be the default search provider on the Safari browser, a topic that I discussed in a recent coverage), however, the company can still pay to be the default so long as it’s non-exclusive.

In essence, this was a massive win for the US tech sector (especially for Google and Apple). Not binding here, but this shows where the remedial winds are blowing for Big Tech cases in the US.

To be clear, a divestiture of Instagram and/or WhatsApp could carry significant downside risk for the topline of the company.

Let me start with Instagram. To put into perspective how important this segment is to the topline, I considered including some analyst estimates. They indicate that this year, Instagram will generate $32 billion in US ad revenue, up 24.4% over 2024.

According to the report, the US average revenue per Instagram user (on an annualized basis) is $250. To put that figure into perspective, the estimate for Facebook is only $207, TikTok $132, and YouTube $40.

Furthermore, Instagram could get a significant boost in its user base if (big if) a TikTok ban takes effect in the US. In this scenario, Reels could see a significant improvement in ad revenue. Ex-TikTok ban, analysts estimate that Reels (helped by Threads) could represent a combined 9.6% of the topline of Meta's worldwide revenue.

I considered including below how Meta's DAP metric evolved over the last few years.

Considering that Instagram could have 2 billion monthly active users and 500 million daily active users, the divestiture would be a disaster for the company.

Moving on to WhatsApp, back in Q1 this year, Meta confirmed 3 billion monthly active users, with 100 million based in the US. Furthermore, the company reported 1.5 billion daily active users of the updates tab (i.e., status + channels). That said, WhatsApp has a smaller weight to the topline of the company. In Q2 2025, the "Family of Apps other revenue" was $583 million, up 50% yoy, driven by WhatsApp paid messaging revenue growth as well as Meta Verified subscriptions.

In my view, the upside that WhatsApp presents to the bull case is from future revenue growth (not past performance). The rollout of ads in status and channels, along with channel subscriptions in the updates tab, has decent potential for the top line. However, CFO Susan Li noted the following in the last earnings call:

We also expect WhatsApp ads and status to earn a lower average price than Facebook or Instagram ads for the foreseeable future, due in part towards WhatsApp skew toward lower monetizing markets, and more limited information that can be used for targeting.

Overall, the upside (especially to the bottom line) could be limited as these ads are served outside of the US.

In my view, the biggest upside that WhatsApp brings to the company is its place in the company's ecosystem of apps. A good number of Facebook or Instagram ads open a live chat on WhatsApp after a user clicks on them. Back in 2023, the company said this ad format (click to message) surpassed a $10B annual run rate. Unfortunately, Meta hasn’t published a newer, official figure, although I don't think it would be a stretch to assume a double-digit CAGR.

To finalize this section, the final ruling on September 2 required Google to share certain search data with rivals to help competition. While I think this will help Bing (owned by Microsoft) significantly more than Meta, I still see some upside. A closer look at Google's search algorithm could reveal key insights for Meta's own recommendation algorithm in its family of apps.

Overall, I feel encouraged by Google's recent win.

Still A Buy?

Since my last article, the stock is down by mid-single digits. As seen below, the share price has pulled back to a previous resistance (now support) level.

This is a good sign, particularly as the RSI has now cooled off below 50. Speaking of metrics cooling off, valuation ratios have (slightly) cooled off. Investors are now paying 26x for next year's earnings. Even though this multiple is still above the sector median (by 42%), I believe the premium is more than justified, considering the growth prospects in the family of apps segment.

As noted below, the Street is projecting growth in the high teens for the next quarters.

Considering Meta's market cap of $1.85 trillion, I believe these growth rates justify the premium on the share price.

Looking Ahead

I believe the pullback in August presents a decent entry opportunity for a long position in this stock. I expect the FTC case to reach a final decision in the near term. A non-divestiture remedy would likely unlock multiple expansion, while any structural separation, particularly for Instagram, would be a disaster for the top line of the company. Speaking of the top line, continued Reels ad load gains, click-to-message momentum in WhatsApp, and Threads engagement and ad rollout are catalysts, with TikTok-ban optionality as a nice upside. Overall, I reiterate my strong buy rating.

data.title: AMD: Why P/E 100 Isn't As Crazy As It Seems
data.description:
At $151 per share, Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) is now worth $263 billion. But its revenue this year is estimated at a modest $29 billion, and its profit at $2.8 billion. With such indicators, the P/E level is 90-100. And at the same time, it is worth noting that in 2021, the company's profit was $3.1 billion, with revenue of $16 billion.

The company's current financial performance is not brilliant! The growth of financial indicators clearly does not correspond to such a high P/E value. But, on the other hand, we see a technology leader that has successfully displaced Intel in the server processor market and is the only company in the world capable of challenging NVIDIA's hegemony in the field of AI chips. And this is what gives the company a high valuation.

In this article, I will try to compare different factors so that you can understand the overall position of the company. Understand what is important and what is not and what gives the company a chance that its high valuation is justified and the company can fulfill the expectations of investors.

AMD's three businesses

The company has three main businesses, but investors are currently betting on only one of them.

The first is their client business, mainly Ryzen processors for personal computers and laptops. In this field, they are waging a fierce battle with Intel. And they have managed to win a significant market share by offering consumers excellent performance at a competitive price. But this market itself is stagnating. The era of explosive growth of the PC has passed. This business is important, it generates stable income, but it cannot be the reason for a P/E under 100.

The second is the gaming segment. This includes their Radeon graphics cards, which compete with NVIDIA GeForce, and their custom chips for Sony PlayStation 5 and Microsoft Xbox gaming consoles. This business is also stable and important because it provides a predictable cash flow for years to come. But it is very cyclical and highly competitive. This segment also cannot justify such a high valuation.

And the third most important business at the moment is the business for data centers. But it can be divided into two parts. The first is their already proven success, EPYC server processors. They really pushed out Intel Xeon and became a new standard for many cloud providers. This is a high-margin and fast-growing business. But the global market for server processors [CPU] in 2025 is estimated at $13-15 billion. Per year. This market will grow by 5-10% and is projected to reach $19 billion by 2030.

The second segment is much more important - the market for AI accelerators [GPU]. In 2025, this market is estimated at $140+ billion. This market is expected to grow explosively to $440 billion by 2030. And AMD's main bet is connected with this market.

Nvidia dominates this market, which I described in one of my articles, already earning huge money, with very fast growth dynamics. And the fact that AMD is a competitor for the most expensive company in the world is key to the current high valuation of the company. After all, if AMD manages to win back at least 5-10% of this market, then its high valuation will be fully justified.

And in this game of investors, given the small current financial indicators of AMD in comparison with its value, it does not matter what happens in the first and second businesses of the company, because their weight in capitalization is insignificant. Investments in AMD are a concentrated bet on one single outcome: will they be able to win back a significant share of the AI ​​accelerator market from NVIDIA. Everything else is unimportant. Therefore, we need to figure this out further.

Hardware Parity and Software Gap

To understand AMD's position in the AI ​​race, we need to divide the battle into two fronts: hardware and software.

I believe that there is no big gap between Nvidia and AMD on the hardware front. Both companies do not have their own chip factories, but order them from TSMC, which makes them on ASML machines. That is, access to manufacturing technologies is the same. Both have brilliant engineers, so plus, minus, the chip engineering itself is also comparable. As a result, their flagship AI accelerators (Instinct from AMD and Blackwell from NVIDIA) are very competitive in pure performance. There is no fundamental, insurmountable gap in the hardware.

The reason why Nvidia dominates is because of software. And here there is not parity between the companies, but a chasm. AMD chips work on their ROCm software, and Nvidia chips on their CUDA software, and they are incompatible with each other.

NVIDIA's CUDA ecosystem can be compared to Microsoft Windows, the operating system that dominates desktop computers. It is the standard for which almost all AI software is written. AMD's ROCm platform is, by analogy, like a new operating system. It may not be worse, but it is incompatible with most programs written for Windows.

You can't force the entire global industry to switch from the familiar CUDA to ROCm. This is an almost impossible task. Due to the huge costs of rewriting programs and retraining specialists.

Where is the Revenue?

And what do we see now? Judging by AMD's past reports. What we see is that at a time when Nvidia is literally collecting money like a vacuum cleaner, AMD's performance is stagnating against this background. And this means that Advanced Micro Devices has not yet managed to ride the wave of growth in GPU sales. And the flow of money is passing it by.

It's already 2025. And the announcement of the release of the AMD Instinct MI300X AI accelerator was in 2023. This was the first chip from AMD that could compete with NVIDIA's flagships in performance. And as the company's management stated then, ROCm became its number 1 priority. That is why, in the spring of 2024, the company's shares soared in price above $200 per share.

Since the spring of 2024, the S&P 500 and NASDAQ have repeatedly rewritten their highs. And in many ways, the growth of the US market was based precisely on the development of the AI ​​industry, but AMD shares were unable to reach new heights. And I think they failed because, in addition to theory and faith, investors are now waiting for at least signals on the practical implementation of the company's plans to conquer the GPU market. But these results are not in sight. While NVIDIA was growing exponentially, AMD's overall performance remained flat. A small increase in the server segment was offset by a decline in the consumer market.

However, the market has shown great patience in evaluating AMD by its future potential. For AMD shares to grow further, promises must turn into real numbers in quarterly reports. Investors need to see evidence that the company has really started to receive billions of dollars from the sale of its AI accelerators. If this does not happen, a painful revaluation of shares downwards is inevitable.

Hope is very strong

But despite the lack of practical results, we must admit that AMD has a very high potential for growth. And most importantly, investors' hope for a bright future has not yet died and probably will not die for a long time. Because despite the seemingly total victory of Nvidia, there is another factor that can play in AMD's favor.

I think that many in the market understand something more important. Investors' hope is based not so much on faith in AMD itself, but on understanding the strategic interests of its customers.

To understand this hope of investors, you need to ask yourself a simple question. What are Microsoft, Google, Meta, and Amazon most afraid of? And they are afraid of the fact that there will be only one supplier of GPUs in the world - the most important component of data centers, and this is NVIDIA.

A situation in which Nvidia will be a monopolist for the entire industry will be a nightmare for the rest. It will be a complete dependence, where the monopolist will dictate its terms.

Therefore, it is beneficial for the tech giants to help AMD. This will be a strategic investment in creating a healthy, competitive market. This will provide an alternative for Nvidia and make them more accommodating. This will be insurance in case of production or logistical problems at NVIDIA. And this will limit the growth of the price of GPUs. They are betting that the world's biggest companies will not let AMD lose this race, because healthy competition in the AI ​​accelerator market is a matter of their own survival and profitability.

Orders and time lag

This theory that giants will support AMD is not just my guess. We are already seeing its confirmation in the real world.

To date, giants such as Microsoft, Meta, Oracle Cloud and major server manufacturers such as Supermicro and Dell have already publicly announced purchases or large-scale implementation of AMD Instinct MI300X AI accelerators.

But here we are faced with the main question that torments investors: "If there are orders, then where is the revenue?" But here the investor must be patient and understand that a lot of time may pass between the announcement of the chip and its mass purchase, which will be reflected in the report.

And this can be understood using a simple model that I will describe. What I will describe is not exact information, but rather an assumption model.

At the end of 2023, AMD announced the MI300X. A major client, such as Microsoft, could receive samples and spend the entire 2024 testing them in their labs: for performance, power consumption, compatibility with their software.

And only at the beginning of 2025, having positive test results, and having an understanding that AMD needs to be supported in order to limit Nvidia's monopoly, which I described above, the first order is made.

Then, AMD, having received the order only at the beginning of 2025, gets in line for the production of these chips at TSMC factories, and plus to this, the production process itself can take 6 months.

As a result, in this model, the first large deliveries of chips for Microsoft will have time to come only in the fall of 2025. And accordingly, AMD's financial indicators will be able to reflect these first deliveries only at the end of the third quarter of 2025 in the best and fastest option, where there were no delays.

Therefore, I assume that now, in September 2025, and further, the first deliveries of GPUs from AMD may begin. Previously, they simply could not appear. But these first deliveries may not be many, and AMD's third-quarter report will probably not have time to show strong financial results either.

But if deliveries have already begun or will begin soon, then information about them may appear in the near future. And if such information appears, it will be a strong signal for AMD shares to grow.

But we must understand that even if the first deliveries begin in the fall or winter of 2025, we will see truly significant, full quarterly results from these deliveries in financial reports only for the first and second quarters of 2026. And this seemingly long period is not a sign of problems, but a normal speed for the world of "hardware".

It is this misunderstanding that creates the current situation - impatient investors, not seeing the results in 2024-2025, are doubtful. And patient investors must understand this cycle and realize that the main catalyst for growth is yet to come.

Risks

But of course I could be wrong, and it is possible that the tech giants are not going to help anyone. An indirect sign of this could be their desire to create their own chips, which they are working on. Then, Nvidia's moat will be impregnable for AMD. And you need to understand that the company's shares have already grown ahead of schedule and the P/E has already reached almost 100. And this suggests that the company's indicators should grow by 2-2.5 times to justify the current share price. This is a serious bar that will be difficult to achieve, and there is a risk that the tech giants will give orders to AMD, but we will be talking about a share of 2-3%, which will increase AMD's financial indicators, but will not be enough to justify today's high valuation.

Summary

As a result, we have AMD with a sky-high P/E ratio, whose current financial performance does not even come close to justifying its high capitalization. But it is the only real competitor in the world for NVIDIA, the most valuable company in the world, which has straddled the fast-growing GPU market.

The strategic interests of the largest technology giants (Microsoft, Meta, Google) require the presence of a strong second player in the AI ​​accelerator market in order to prevent NVIDIA from becoming a total monopoly. It is they, the main consumers of chips, who are interested in AMD's success.

Investors have been waiting for a long time for the company's reports to reflect at least the first successes in conquering the GPU market, but so far there are no such results, which leads to disappointment. But investors need to take into account the long production cycle. From the announcement of the MI300X chip at the end of 2023, through the entirety of 2024 of testing and qualification with customers, to placing orders in early 2025 and their fulfillment at TSMC factories, it may take two years. And this time lag is not a sign of problems, but a normal speed.

I think that most of the testing and production time has already passed, and in the next, probably, six months, AMD's reports will begin to reflect the company's first successes in GPU sales.

And in view of these expectations, I assign AMD shares a Strong Buy rating. As soon as the market sees the first news about the practical implementation of the company's GPU plans, the company's shares will have every reason to go to new heights above $200 per share.

data.title: Academy Sports and Outdoors: Still Cautious About How The Second Half Will Play Out
data.description:
Investment overview

I wrote about Academy Sports and Outdoors, Inc. (ASO) previously with a hold rating, as I wanted to have more clarity on the tariff situation as well as better macro trends. I'm sticking with my hold rating. While there are certainly some encouraging green shoots in its latest quarterly results, my view is that until the uncertainties are cleared, it is not safe to go long. The sales trends still feel a bit choppy, and the inventory position, while strategic, remains a key risk to watch.

2Q25 Earnings Review

Looking at the 2Q25 results, the clear takeaway was the return to positive comp (same-store-sales) growth. After multiple quarters of decline, ASO finally posted a 0.2% comp increase, with total net sales up 3.3% y/y to $1.6 billion. Notably, the comp lift was entirely ticket-driven, with the average basket size up 1.5%, albeit transactions fell 1.4%. E-commerce did well with ~18% y/y growth, a rather strong proof point that digital investments are resonating with customers. Also importantly, the growth was broad-based, as all four major categories (footwear, apparel, sports & recreation, and outdoor) saw low-single-digit growth.

That said, profitability was less encouraging. Gross margin was 36%, which, coupled with higher operating expenses (SG&A deleveraged by 150 bps), led to an earnings miss—adj. EPS came in at $1.94, landing below expectations. Still, management did raise the low end of both sales and EPS guidance for the full year.

Strategic Initiatives Begin to Deliver Tangible Results

I discussed previously that ASO’s execution of its key strategies looked encouraging, but I was not ready to turn bullish because I wanted to see it translate into the numbers. Encouragingly, ASO finally showed proof, marking a clear inflection, in 2Q25. The most important development was the positive comp, and importantly, it was driven by the initiatives I had been watching. For instance, the new store rollout strategy has proven to work. Stores opened in 2022 and 2023 saw their comp trajectory accelerate meaningfully, from low-single-digits in 1Q25 to mid-single-digits in 2Q25. This is a strong indication that the new unit model is not only viable but also scalable, giving me more confidence that ASO has finally found a lever it can pull to drive durable growth.

Aside from that, brand partnerships are something I pointed out previously as well. This strategy is also now paying off in a very tangible way. The launch of the Nike Jordan line was a highlight, with management confirming that Jordan, together with the expanded Nike assortments, delivered “meaningful” double-digit growth. In my view, this has very positive long-term implications. Through this brand launch, ASO has proven its worth as a partner, demonstrating to other premium vendors that it can effectively present their products and drive sales volume. What this creates, I believe, is a flywheel effect; the strong performance with Nike should help ASO continue to gain access to other desirable brands and better product allocations in the future. We're already seeing early signs of this, with ASO already bringing in other new brands like Converse and HydroJug this year.

Core Consumer and Macro Headwinds Remain

Despite the encouraging improvements in sales, I continue to take a cautious stance on ASO. The biggest issue, in my view, is still the health of ASO’s core, lower-income consumer base. I have discussed this before, and I think the situation is still bad. While ASO has done an excellent job of attracting more affluent shoppers (i.e., traffic from households earning >$100,000 was up double-digits), the flipside is that traffic from households earning <$50,000 continued to decline in 2Q25. To be fair, the pace of decline has moderated compared to prior quarters, but nonetheless, it is still a negative trend. This dynamic creates a mixed picture. If we take a step back, the reality seems to be that the positive impact of trade-down from wealthier households is being offset by trade-out from its traditional base, leaving overall demand looking softer than the headline numbers suggest.

This softness is also visible in the volatility of sales cadence. While the back-to-school season was solid for ASO, management noted that August-to-date sales had slipped slightly negative once the event passed. What this suggests to me is that consumers are still in the phase of “episodic” shopping, where consumers concentrate their spending around major promotions but pull back in between. In other words, is underlying demand really improving? Or is it simply because there was a promotion going on? This makes forecasting the near-term growth trajectory difficult and paints a rather uncertain underlying demand picture.

ASO’s elevated inventory position certainly doesn’t give comfort. Merchandise inventories increased 16.2% y/y in 2Q25. Management justified this as a strategic move to pull forward evergreen products ahead of tariff impacts. While I agree that this reduces markdown exposure, the sheer size of the increase in this choppy demand environment still carries risk. Should 2H25 sales momentum fall short, ASO could be forced into more aggressive promotions, putting renewed pressure on margins.

Valuation

I still think it is tough to give ASO a buy rating. There are certainly good things going on, which probably work out well over the long term (like the partnership strategy), but I am not so sure about the underlying demand health here. Core consumer spending is certainly declining, and to make things worse, ASO is now in an elevated inventory position. The stock is also not trading at a cheap valuation vs. history. It is at 8x forward PE now, which is ~0.5x above the past 3-year average of 7.5x. This is not a steep premium, but it certainly doesn’t provide a good margin of safety. Be reminded that ASO traded as low as 6x in April, so there is precedent for multiples to go a lot lower should sales momentum fall.

Conclusion

In conclusion, my rating for ASO is a hold. 2Q25 offered clear proof that strategic initiatives are gaining traction, with store rollout and brand partnerships delivering tangible results. However, the underlying demand picture remains fragile, weighed down by weakness in the lower-income core consumer. Elevated inventory further clouds the near-term outlook, and the stock is not trading at an especially attractive valuation versus history. Until I see stronger signs of durable demand recovery, I think ASO will likely stay range-bound rather than re-rate higher.

data.title: SCAP: Seeking To Reducing Small-Cap Volatility While Generating Higher Income
data.description:
Plenty has been said about the flaws of passive small-cap ETFs that track the Russell 2000. One of the biggest issues is the index’s sheer breadth and relatively loose inclusion criteria, which allow hundreds of “zombie” companies (businesses with little to no profitability) to remain in the mix.

That in turn contributes to another common knock against the index: volatility. Because of their size and weaker fundamentals, many Russell 2000 constituents can swing wildly, making the index much more volatile than large-cap benchmarks like the S&P 500. For some investors, that’s reason enough to avoid small caps altogether.

We believe the InfraCap Small Cap Income ETF (NYSEARCA:SCAP) offers a more thoughtful alternative. By combining actively managed, value-conscious stock selection with the ability to tactically allocate to preferred shares and write options, SCAP is designed to potentially dampen volatility while boosting income.

SCAP: Seeking Lower Volatility

SCAP doesn’t attempt to replicate the full investable universe of U.S. small caps. Instead, the strategy narrows the field by focusing on three attributes that historically correlate with stronger fundamentals and lower volatility.

First is profitability. Some small-cap stocks generate little to no earnings, which adds to their volatility and lowers the reliability of future returns. SCAP, in contrast, targets companies with positive earnings and durable margins. These are businesses with demonstrated financial discipline and a clearer path to compounding returns over time.

Second is the presence of a dividend. Dividend payers are relatively rare in the small-cap space, so identifying them often reveals businesses with healthier cash flows, more mature operations, and shareholder-friendly capital allocation. It also adds an income component to the portfolio that can help cushion volatility.

Finally, SCAP emphasizes valuation. Target holdings must trade at reasonable multiples of both growth and earnings. That avoids speculative small caps with sky-high price-to-sales ratios or structurally unprofitable business models that rely on capital markets to survive.

Taken together, this approach filters out the weakest links and concentrates the portfolio in higher-quality small caps: businesses that are generating cash, returning capital, and not simply surviving on promises.

Over the period shown in the chart below, that quality tilt has translated into consistently lower volatility for SCAP compared to ETFs tracking the Russell 2000 index, even during dislocations like April/May 2025's tariff-induced selloff.

SCAP: Seeking Higher Yield

The Russell 2000 Index isn’t known for its income potential. Many of the small-cap stocks it includes are still in the early stages of development, meaning they often lack the free cash flow or stability required to support a regular dividend. As a result, ETFs tracking this benchmark may only offer a yield of around 1%, give or take.

In contrast, SCAP pays a 6.17% 30-day SEC yield as of 6/30/25. Part of that is due to its dividend-focused screening process, which favors profitable small caps that already return capital to shareholders. But SCAP also structurally enhances its income profile through two additional sources.

The first is options writing. Even though the portfolio itself is actively managed and differs meaningfully from the Russell 2000, SCAP opportunistically writes index options on the Russell 2000 or ETFs to capture volatility premiums. This allows the fund to tap into the higher implied volatility of the benchmark without owning many of the lower-quality names responsible for it.

The second is modest leverage. SCAP can deploy between 10% and 30% notional leverage to amplify exposure to dividend-paying holdings and increase capital allocated to its covered call strategy. This boosts distributable income while maintaining exposure to higher-quality businesses.

While yield isn’t the whole story, SCAP’s advantage isn’t limited to income alone. As shown in the chart below, it also led the Russell 2000 ETF (IWM) on a total return basis over the past year, with distributions reinvested, as of 8/8/25.
